The shortest path that visits all nodes is called the Hamiltonian path, if one exists that visits each node exactly once. If no such path exists, it becomes the "shortest path that visits all nodes" problem, which is a variant of the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) and is NP-hard.

However, the problem description says "visit all the rooms with the fewest number of room changes possible". It doesn't say I can't revisit rooms. It just asks for the *fewest number of room changes* from the *current state* to *visit all remaining unvisited rooms*. This is different from a simple DFS or BFS which might re-traverse many edges to find all nodes. The shortest path that visits all nodes is called the Hamiltonian path, if one exists that visits each node exactly once. If no such path exists, it becomes the "shortest path that visits all nodes" problem, which is a variant of the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) and is NP-hard.
